# Cross-Project Learnings

**Temporary holding pen.** Learnings land here first, but nothing should stay. Every entry should be promoted to where it will actually be read and enacted at the right moment — the relevant CLAUDE.md, Hub/spoke, protocol doc, build doc, or ADR (with proper breadcrumbing so the ADR gets discovered at the right moment, not buried). Once promoted, condense to a one-line stub. Stubs stay for cross-pollination to new repos or contexts.

**Do not edit this file without Justin's approval.** Propose changes; don't make them directly.

*Last updated: 2026-03-09*

---

## Unpromoted

46. **Strip Instructions from Data Files.** If a file is read every session but only written to occasionally, don't put writing instructions in the file — put them in the protocol that gates writing. The file stays clean (lower token cost on every load), and the instructions are in context exactly when they're needed. The file itself should have a guardrail warning not to write or edit without reading the protocol first — otherwise it will almost certainly get overwritten by an eager agent. Applied to agent MEMORY.md files: writing guidance moved to #lock protocol, consolidation guidance to #consolidate protocol. *Promote to:* Already applied in team workspace. Consider for other repos where instruction-heavy files are loaded on every session.

47. **Anchor-then-Expand for Web Research.** When researching a person to ground an interaction, fetch the 1-2 most identity-specific URLs first to lock identity (name collisions are real — e.g., "Mark Titus" the filmmaker vs. unrelated namesakes). Only then fan out via web search for color, currency, and third-party perspective. Skipping the anchor step lets common-name results pollute the profile. Empirically (2026-04-27 subagent test on Mark Titus): WebFetch alone on the two anchor sites = 5/10 richness in 27 calls; WebSearch+WebFetch with anchor-first = 9/10 in 8 calls. The unlock isn't just "more sources" — it's recent activity, third-party perspective, and the inside details (etymology, biographical specifics) that almost never live on the anchor sites. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Mira incorporated into evryn-backend `identity/activities/onboarding.md` "Look Them Up" section (commits 3771ca8 + 416cd44).

48. **Capture Inside Details — Highest Signal-Per-Token.** When research surfaces "inside" details — etymology, side mentions, biographical specifics that almost never live on first-party bio pages (e.g., "Eva's Wild" = "save" reversed; the boat the founder filmed on; sobriety arc) — these are the highest-signal-per-token facts for a relationship broker. They're what makes the difference between "she read his website" (cool) and "she actually understands his trajectory in the world" (wow). Generic facts feel like research; inside details feel like knowing. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Mira incorporated into onboarding.md "Hunt for inside details" beat.

49. **Hold Research with Humility — Breadcrumbs, Not Firehose.** A research-armed agent that dumps everything she found is a stalker. Breadcrumbs feel like attentiveness — bring up one or two things naturally, ask-don't-state when uncertain, name the source when surfacing a fact. Discretion shapes the *how* of using research, not the *whether*. (Source: Mira's onboarding.md edit, Nathan-influenced.) *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS.

50. **No Tiering Research by User Importance.** AC's instinct was to tier research by relationship importance ("light for casual, deeper for gatekeepers/key matches"). Justin's pushback (via Mira): every user is center-of-universe at the user level. The question isn't *whether* they deserve research but the *posture* with which you carry what you find. Discipline lives in humility and discretion, not in a VIP-vs-not hierarchy. Tiering imports a model antithetical to Evryn's "every person matters" framing. *Promoted to:* AGENT_PATTERNS. Implicit in Mira's onboarding.md framing.

51. **Trigger Handoff Should Offer Structured Response Options, Not Just Force One Default.** v0.2 architecture says "one code path, not two — Evryn classifies intent." Elegant in principle. But the trigger handoff prompt currently *instructs* Evryn to `submit_draft` on every inbound email — there's no built-in concept of "no response needed" or "this is system noise, just log and move on." Result: when Google sent a system notification (no human recipient) during the 2026-04-27 integration test prep, Evryn had no escape hatch and went into a doomed-to-fail draft loop. The tactical fix shipped in the same DC bundle (give her a `notify_slack` exit for no-reply senders); the deeper architectural question — should the trigger handoff offer Evryn a structured set of response options (draft / escalate / ignore-silently) rather than instruction-followed-by-escape-hatch? — is a future #lock + ADR conversation. *Promote to:* potentially evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md as an open design question. Filed in DC mailbox backlog and `SPRINT-MARK-LIVE.md` Backlog section (entry dated 2026-04-27).

52. **Evryn's Judgment Holds Under Pressure (Calibration Data Point).** During the 2026-04-27 loop bug, Evryn (1) classified the verification-code emails correctly as `ignore` upfront, (2) made a defensible call on the security alert (escalate to human via edge classification), (3) **diagnosed the runtime root cause herself** without prompting (her draft 22:06: "It's one of my own prior draft submissions being logged as direct_message... self-propagating loop"), (4) used `submit_draft` as a back-channel ("[Note for Justin — REJECT]") since drafts were her only outbound to him, (5) pinged Slack in parallel to surface outside the clogged draft channel, (6) eventually broke the loop herself via `supabase_upsert` when she saw rejections weren't catching up, (7) stayed in voice the entire time — direct, unpanicky, no apology spiral. The bugs were architectural; her response was good. **Important calibration: her judgment is solid even when the system around her is broken.** Worth holding as a data point when calibrating trust in her over time. *Promote to:* possibly into the trust-calibration framework when one exists. For now, captured in `_evryn-meta/docs/sessions/2026-04-27-integration-test-pivot-and-loop-bug.md`.

53. **System Actors Are FK Anchors and Senders, Never Subjects.** Evryn and the Operator have user records so the messages table has valid `sender_id` / `recipient_id` values when Evryn sends outbound or the Operator messages Evryn on Slack — they're legitimately senders and recipients in those flows. What they should *never* be is the *subject* of a user-scoped operation: profile loading via `buildPersonContext`, history loading via `getRecentMessages(systemActorId)` (which returns every conversation in the system because system actors are involved in all of them), proactive outreach enrollment, etc. These operations assume "the queried user is a person Evryn might want to know more about" — a frame that doesn't apply to FK anchors. The Slack-to-Evryn flow already follows this correctly: when the Operator messages Evryn, the handler stores the message with `sender_id = OPERATOR_USER_ID` (correct), but composes the prompt from a fixed Operator context string and *doesn't* call `getRecentMessages` — system actor is sender, never subject. The 2026-04-27 loop bug was the inbound email path violating this principle: Evryn's own polled-back outbound resolved to her own user record, and the pipeline subject-ified it (built person-context, queried history). The fix is at the gate (don't let a system actor resolve as inbound sender), not at history loading downstream. The originally-proposed "skip history loading when sender is system actor" was wrong altitude — defense-in-depth at the wrong layer adds runtime branching for a state that shouldn't arise. *Promote to:* evryn-backend ARCHITECTURE.md (Data Model — System Actors section) pending Justin's approval. Principle worth stating durably.

54. **Defense-in-Depth Layers Should Not Cause Workflow Friction.** The `getRecipient()` redirect was originally a primary safety layer (protect against accidentally emailing real Mark). Over time, real-Mark's email was verified absent everywhere, Mark in DB became `systemtest@evryn.ai`, and other safety layers (approval gate, kill switch, identity-layer escalation exit) closed the same risk. The redirect became a fix-on-fix that actively broke the review@ workflow during testing — drafts intended for Justin's review inbox got redirected away. **Lesson:** when stronger safety layers land, don't keep older defense-in-depth layers if they cause active friction. Periodically re-evaluate whether each layer earns its operational cost. *Promote to:* AC CLAUDE.md Architectural Mandate, possibly. Captured in ADR-029 (`docs/decisions/029-remove-getrecipient-redirect.md`, 2026-04-27).

*New learnings land here temporarily before being promoted to their permanent homes.*
